content.full_text:
• CITY OF

CITY OF EL SEGUNDO

EL SEGUNDO

AUG 2 2 1990

ALAN WEST, Councilman

August 22, 1990

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
John G. McLean

Re: FPPC No. 90/126
    Alan West

Dear John McLean:

I received your letter dated August 16 and I am glad that you came
to a decision that favored my position. Based on all the facts that
you mentioned it seemed only normal that this is the way the case
would turn out, again I thank you.

I now ask you a rhetorical question based on the same set of facts
and I would very much appreciate it if you would take the time to
answer as it will help me in future issues affecting downtown.
In your opinion no mention was made about, Effects on General
Public Exclusion, (FPPC Section 18703.)"...there is no conflict of
interest unless the effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.." Main Street downtown is about four blocks
long, our residential town is about one square mile area and down
town is approximately in the center. My understanding of the law
is that downtown decisions affect all people of the town, and more
importantly all merchants are effected the same. Please look at
your own case (2 FPPC ops 77.) Ownership of a business inside of
a redevelopment did not constitute a conflict because it had the
same effect on all the merchants. I can understand if a decision
was made about the sidewalk directly in front of only one shop that
it would most certainly be in conflict, but decisions about
downtown in general would seem to affect everyone the same and
thereby exempting me from conflict. I could of course abstain and
not vote or discuss downtown issues but I honestly believe that
would be a "cop out", as a small town councilman it's my duty to
make important discussion about our town and many of them are
connected with downtown.

Our city attorney sits at every meeting and he is connected with
one of the biggest city government law offices in California, is
not his advice valid? Its my understanding that if I go directly
against his advice that subsequently causes a liability I can
become liable personally, it almost seems that I'm "damned if I do
and damned if I don't". I sent you a copy of the minutes showing
his approval about my vote on a downtown issue, however at the end

350 Main Street    El Segundo, California 90245    (213) 322-4670

Page 2
Case # 90/126
Alan West

of your letter you state, "..however this matter will be considered
when evaluating future alleged violation of the act". You can surly
see that my political opponents will view this statement with great
satisfaction, they will be able "to get me", the next time that
they turn me in for a similar alleged violation. We often have a
split vote on these issues so my opinion sometimes is the deciding
factor. I do hope that you can fully appreciate my interest and
not do what our city attorney so often does, that is render a very
broad opinion the could be interpreted most any way.

I believe that as the state watchdog agency you owe me a definitive
answer on this issue so that there is no question on future issues
that come up, as I stated earlier downtown is a frequent city
government discussion and I want a final and permanent answer from
you, do I discuss and vote on downtown issues or not??

Thanks again for your confidence in my good judgment.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Alan West

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilman's ownership of a business in downtown can create a conflict of interest when voting on general downtown development issues that affect all merchants equally, and whether he can lawfully participate in such votes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence from the subject (Alan West) responding to an FPPC advice letter (90/126) and asking a follow-up question. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections are present. The document is argumentative in nature rather than a structured request. FPPC has not yet responded to this inquiry.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether a city councilman's ownership of a business in downtown can create a conflict of interest when voting on general downtown development issues that affect all merchants equally, and whether he can lawfully participate in such votes.

embedding.summary: A city councilman requests clarification from the FPPC regarding conflict of interest rules for voting on downtown issues where his business is located. He argues that since such decisions affect all downtown merchants equally, they should not constitute a conflict under FPPC section 18703, and he seeks a definitive answer to guide his future voting.
